title: Cat Tremors and Muscle Twitching: A Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what cat tremors and muscle twitching mean, why they are often emergencies, and how to document symptoms to help your veterinarian diagnose.
content_html:
TL;DR: Muscle tremors and twitching in cats are high-priority symptoms that often indicate toxin exposure or metabolic issues and require immediate veterinary attention. Capturing a video of the episode can provide critical information to help your veterinarian reach an accurate diagnosis quickly.

What are the signs of tremors and muscle twitching in cats?

Tremors and muscle twitching in cats are involuntary, rhythmic muscle contractions that can range from localized (a single limb or the face) to generalized (the entire body). While occasional skin twitching can be normal, persistent shaking or uncontrollable movements usually indicate an underlying neurological, metabolic, or toxicological issue that requires immediate attention.

When should I seek emergency care for my cat’s tremors?

  • The urgency level for cat tremors is High; contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if symptoms occur.
  • Seek immediate help if twitching is accompanied by confusion, drooling, vomiting, or a high body temperature.
  • Common causes include exposure to toxins (such as lilies or permethrin), low blood sugar, or severe electrolyte imbalances.
  • Professional intervention is necessary to prevent potential seizures or permanent physiological damage.

How can recording a video help the vet diagnose my cat's tremors?

  • A video captures symptoms that may stop during a clinic visit due to the cat's adrenaline.
  • Veterinary teams can observe the exact pattern, frequency, and severity of movements in the home environment.
  • Documentation helps the vet distinguish between tremors, focal seizures, or behavioral responses, leading to a faster diagnosis.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Muscle tremors and twitching in cats can stem from various causes, including toxicities from insecticides such as chlorinated hydrocarbons and organophosphates, where clinical signs can range from trembling to muscle twitching, convulsions, and even coma. Organophosphate toxicity can manifest as muscle tremors and twitching, generalized muscle weakness, tetraparesis, and ventroflexion of the neck, sometimes without initial signs of acute toxicity. Feline hypokalemic polymyopathy, resulting from excessive urinary potassium depletion or inadequate dietary intake, can also cause generalized muscle weakness and ventroflexion of the neck.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3062)

faqs.question: Could my cat just be cold?
faqs.answer: While cats do shiver if they are hypothermic, true muscle tremors are usually distinct from shivering and often occur regardless of the room temperature. If they are shivering and also acting lethargic, seek medical care.

faqs.question: Can certain flea treatments cause twitching?
faqs.answer: Yes, products containing pyrethroids or permethrins (common in dog treatments) are highly toxic to cats and frequently cause severe muscle tremors and seizures.

faqs.question: What should I do while waiting to get to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a quiet, dark, and padded area (like a carrier with blankets) to prevent injury from falls. Do not try to give them food or water as they may choke.
